abstract:
Characterizing the elemental composition of organic aerosol (OA) is essential for understanding its sources, chemical transformation, and overall environmental impact. The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) has become a primary tool for these measurements. In this study, we re-evaluate AMS metrics—including oxygen-to-carbon (O:C), hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C), and organic mass-to-organic carbon (OM:OC) ratios, as well as carbon oxidation state ($\text{OS}_\text{C}$)—using a significantly expanded laboratory dataset of multifunctional organic standards. 

We found that the "Aiken-Explicit" method, which utilizes all measured ion intensities, captures O:C and H:C ratios within 20% and 12% of known values, respectively. However, the more standard "Aiken-Ambient" approach—which estimates $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$ and $\text{CO}^+$ intensities to avoid atmospheric interferences—tends to systematically underestimate these ratios, particularly for alcohols and organic acids. Our analysis of $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$, $\text{CO}^+$, and $\text{CO}_2^+$ fragments, supported by combined AMS and vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization experiments, reveals that these ions primarily result from dehydration and decarboxylation on the $600^\circ\text{C}$ AMS vaporizer. Notably, this thermal decomposition remains efficient even at temperatures as low as $200^\circ\text{C}$.

To address these systematic biases, we propose an "Improved-Ambient" elemental analysis method. By using specific ion fragments as markers for molecular functionality, this method reduces errors and provides a more accurate representation of complex organic mixtures. When applied to ambient OOA, the Improved-Ambient method yields O:C and H:C ratios that are 27% and 11% higher, respectively, than those derived from previous methods. These findings suggest that ambient aerosols are more oxygenated than previously thought. Interestingly, $\text{OS}_\text{C}$ values remained consistent across methods, identifying it as a more robust metric for tracking OA oxidation since it is inherently less sensitive to the effects of water loss or gain during atmospheric processing or instrumental analysis.
